statement.markdown: Given two strings s and t, return the number of distinct subsequences of s which equals t. A subsequence of a string is a new string that is formed from the original string by deleting some (can be none) of the characters without disturbing the relative positions of the remaining characters. (i.e., "ace" is a subsequence of "abcde" while "aec" is not).

hints.text: Think about how you can form a subsequence from string s that matches string t. Consider the role of each character in s and how it can contribute to forming t.

Realize that this problem can be solved using dynamic programming. Consider creating a 2D array where one dimension represents the characters in string s and the other dimension represents the characters in string t.

The dynamic programming state dp[i][j] can represent the number of distinct subsequences of s[:i] that equals t[:j]. Think about how to transition from one state to another, especially when the current characters in s and t match or not.

editorial.title: Distinct Subsequences
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the number of distinct subsequences in string s that equals string t. A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subsequences of s and checking if they match t, which is inefficient due to the large number of subsequences.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would have a time complexity of O(2^n * m) where n is the length of string s and m is the length of string t, making it impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The observation that unlocks the optimal approach is that this problem has overlapping subproblems, making dynamic programming (DP) a suitable method for solving it.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses dynamic programming with a time complexity of O(n * m) and a space complexity of O(m), where n is the length of string s and m is the length of string t.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the DP approach can be proven by observing that dp[i][j] represents the number of distinct subsequences in s[0..i] that equals t[0..j]. If s[i] equals t[j], then dp[i][j] equals dp[i-1][j-1] (the new subsequence) plus dp[i-1][j] (the old subsequences). If s[i] does not equal t[j], then dp[i][j] equals dp[i-1][j]. The base case dp[0][0] equals 1 (one way to match an empty string with an empty string).
editorial.dryRun: The provided Python code implements this DP approach efficiently, iterating over string s and updating the dp array in reverse order to avoid overwriting previously computed values.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly initializing the DP array, leading to incorrect base cases.
Failing to iterate over string s and t in the correct order, leading to incorrect updates of the DP array.
Not handling the case when s[i] does not equal t[j] correctly, leading to incorrect counts.

functionSignature.java: public int numDistinct(String s, String t);
functionSignature.python: def numDistinct(self, s: str, t: str) -> int:
